stem: Use Euler's formula \( e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta \) to evaluate \( e^{i\pi [[k]]/6} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: By Euler's formula \( e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta \) with \( \theta = \dfrac{\pi [[k]]}{6} \), we get \( e^{i\pi [[k]]/6} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of sine and cosine; the real part is \( \cos\theta \), not \( \sin\theta \).
This is \( e^{-i\theta} \) (the conjugate); the sign of the imaginary part was flipped.
This is \( -e^{i\theta} = e^{i(\theta+\pi)} \), a rotation by an extra half turn.
This is \( i\,e^{i\theta} = e^{i(\theta+\pi/2)} \), a rotation by an extra quarter turn.
This is off by one in the real part.
This is off by one in the real part.

stem: Use Euler's formula to write \( e^{[[a]] + i\pi [[k]]/6} \) in the rectangular form \( x + yi \), where \( x \) and \( y \) may involve the constant \( e \).
solution: Split the exponent: \( e^{[[a]]+i\pi[[k]]/6} = e^{[[a]]}\,e^{i\pi[[k]]/6} = e^{[[a]]}\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi[[k]]}{6} + i\sin\dfrac{\pi[[k]]}{6}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the scaling factor \( e^{[[a]]} \) entirely.
This swaps sine and cosine; the real part must scale \( \cos\theta \).
This is the conjugate; the sign of the imaginary part was flipped.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Given \( e^{i\pi [[k]]/6} \), use the law of exponents for complex exponentials to compute \( \left(e^{i\pi [[k]]/6}\right)^{[[m]]} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: By the exponent rule \( (e^{i\theta})^{m} = e^{im\theta} \) with \( \theta=\pi[[k]]/6 \) and \( m=[[m]] \), the power equals \( \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi[[k]]\cdot[[m]]}{6}\right) + i\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi[[k]]\cdot[[m]]}{6}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to scale the angle by \( m \), leaving \( \theta \) unchanged.
This multiplies the whole exponential by \( m \) instead of scaling the angle.
This is the conjugate; the sign of the imaginary part was flipped.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using Euler's formula, \( \cos\theta = \dfrac{e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta}}{2} \). Evaluate \( \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi [[k]]}{6}\right) \) using this identity.
solution: By Euler's formula, \( e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta} = 2\cos\theta \), so \( \cos\theta = \dfrac{e^{i\theta}+e^{-i\theta}}{2} \). With \( \theta = \pi[[k]]/6 \), this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the difference identity \( \dfrac{e^{i\theta}-e^{-i\theta}}{2} \), which gives \( i\sin\theta \), not \( \cos\theta \).
This is \( e^{i\theta} \) itself, not the averaged combination that isolates \( \cos\theta \).
This computes \( \sin\theta \) instead of \( \cos\theta \).
This negates the correct value (a sign slip).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the exponential law \( e^{i\alpha}e^{i\beta} = e^{i(\alpha+\beta)} \), compute \( e^{i\pi [[k1]]/6}\cdot e^{i\pi [[k2]]/6} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: By the exponential law, the product of the two exponentials adds their angles: \( e^{i\pi[[k1]]/6}e^{i\pi[[k2]]/6} = e^{i\pi([[k1]]+[[k2]])/6} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi([[k1]]+[[k2]])}{6}\right) + i\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi([[k1]]+[[k2]])}{6}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by an extra additive constant from a miscomputed product.
This subtracts the angles instead of adding them.
This is the conjugate; the sign of the imaginary part was flipped.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
